original_hypothesis.title: onlyOperator Modifier Inverted Logic Locks Out All Designated Operators
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: The onlyOperator modifier uses `if (msg.sender != owner() || operator[msg.sender]) revert` which reverts when operator[msg.sender] is true. This means designated operators can NEVER call harvest, delegateAmount, undelegateAmount, redelegateAmount, harvestAndDelegateRewards, or finaliseRonRewardsForEpoch. Additionally, if the owner is also set as an operator via updateOperator(owner, true), even the owner is permanently locked out of all operator functions until the mapping is toggled back.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The boolean condition `msg.sender != owner() || operator[msg.sender]` evaluates to true (reverts) whenever operator[msg.sender] is true, regardless of ownership. The intended logic is clearly `msg.sender != owner() && !operator[msg.sender]` — allowing either the owner OR an operator to proceed. This is a critical access control defect affecting all staking management functions.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (msg.sender != owner() || operator[msg.sender]) revert — OR operator should use AND with negation: != owner() && !operator[msg.sender]

preconditions: An address is set as operator via updateOperator(addr, true)
That operator attempts to call any onlyOperator-guarded function

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Incorrect boolean logic in access control modifier
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: operator[msg.sender] being true causes revert instead of allowing access
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No test coverage for operator-as-non-owner calling gated functions

analysis_questions: Does the deployment script set any operators? (No — only owner deploys and calls directly)
Can the owner still call operator functions? (Yes, only if operator[owner] is false)
If owner calls updateOperator(owner, true), are all operator functions bricked?
validation_requirements: Test: set operator[addr]=true, have addr call harvest() — expect revert
Test: set operator[owner]=true, have owner call delegateAmount() — expect revert
Verify no alternate code path bypasses the modifier

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: After deployment, if the owner sets a bot as operator and assumes the bot handles harvesting/finalization, all staking management stops. User withdrawal requests via requestWithdrawal cannot be finalized. The owner must directly manage all operations without being flagged as operator.
prerequisites: Owner sets at least one operator address via updateOperator

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Total protocol paralysis if owner is also set as operator; delegation of staking ops impossible otherwise
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All protocol users — staking cannot be managed by designated operators
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Owner can toggle operator[owner]=false to regain access, but designated operators remain permanently locked out

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No test verifying that non-owner operators can call operator-gated functions

technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Immediate on any operator function call

validation_result.impact_summary: Designated operators are permanently locked out of all staking and reward management functions. The protocol can only be managed by the owner.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The `onlyOperator` modifier in `LiquidRon.sol` is intended to restrict access to either the contract owner or a designated operator. However, the boolean logic used is `if (msg.sender != owner() || operator[msg.sender]) revert ErrInvalidOperator();`. If a designated operator (who is not the owner) calls the function, `operator[msg.sender]` is `true`, making the entire `||` condition `true`, which triggers the revert. Consequently, no designated operator can ever successfully call functions protected by this modifier (such as `harvest`, `delegateAmount`, `finaliseRonRewardsForEpoch`, etc.). The only address that can bypass this modifier is the owner, provided the owner has not been added to the `operator` mapping.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The code evidence clearly shows the logical flaw in the modifier. The use of the logical OR (`||`) instead of logical AND (`&&`) combined with the negation of the operator check (`!operator[msg.sender]`) results in an inverted access control mechanism.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: The condition evaluates to true whenever `operator[msg.sender]` is true, causing the transaction to revert for any valid operator. The intended logic should be `msg.sender != owner() && !operator[msg.sender]`.

execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: Inside the `onlyOperator` modifier, the condition `msg.sender != owner() || operator[msg.sender]` is evaluated. Since the caller is an operator, `operator[msg.sender]` is true. The condition evaluates to true, and the transaction reverts with `ErrInvalidOperator()`, preventing the operator from executing the function.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: The deployment script `LiquidRon_saigon.s.sol` does not set any operators. The owner calls `delegateAmount` directly. Since the owner is not an operator, `operator[owner]` is false, and the modifier passes (`false || false` -> `false`). This explains why the bug does not block deployment but will manifest as soon as an operator is designated.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The scenario is highly realistic and requires no complex exploitation. It is a fundamental functional defect. If the protocol relies on a bot or a separate operational address to harvest rewards or finalize epochs, those operations will fail. The owner must perform all actions directly, defeating the purpose of the operator role.
validation_result.impact_assessment: High operational impact. It breaks the intended role-based access control, forcing the owner (which might be a cold wallet or multisig) to perform routine, time-sensitive operations like harvesting and finalizing epochs. If the owner accidentally sets themselves as an operator, all operations are locked until they unset themselves.
recommended_mitigations: Correct the boolean logic in the `onlyOperator` modifier to use logical AND and negate the operator check: `if (msg.sender != owner() && !operator[msg.sender]) revert ErrInvalidOperator();`
validation_result.replication_plan:
1. Deploy LiquidRon.
2. Call `updateOperator(operatorAddress, true)` from the owner.
3. Attempt to call `harvest(0, [])` from `operatorAddress`.
4. Observe the transaction revert with `ErrInvalidOperator()`.
additional_testing_needed: Implement unit tests that explicitly verify access control for both the owner and designated operators across all restricted functions.
